pattern: With only 5 possible digits, trying every ordered pair as (odd-frequency digit, even-frequency digit) and tracking, per parity signature of the two running prefix counts, the best prefix seen so far — released only once it is both far enough back and from an already-closed count bucket for the even digit — turns the substring search into a handful of linear scans.
hints: For a fixed choice of digit a (wanting an odd count) and digit b (wanting an even, nonzero count), define f(i) = (count of a in the first i characters) - (count of b in the first i characters). The best substring [l, r) has value f(r) - f(l), and needs the parity of a's count to differ between l and r, the parity of b's count to match between l and r, and at least one b strictly between l and r.

The 'at least one b in between' condition is naturally satisfied by only ever comparing a prefix l against later prefixes once b's running count has moved on from l's bucket — delay adding a prefix into the tracked minimum until the next b appears after it, AND until the window length reaches k, whichever happens later.

For each of the 20 ordered (a, b) pairs with a != b, scan left to right maintaining the minimum f(l) for each of the 4 (parity of a, parity of b) signatures among prefixes that are already eligible; take the best f(r) - f(l) with opposite a-parity and matching b-parity across every position and every pair.

edge_inputs.description: minimum length equal to k, only the whole string is a candidate
k is 1, every substring length is allowed
the globally best pair of digits only appears in a substring shorter than k
the even-frequency digit must actually occur, a zero-count digit cannot be chosen

stress.description: random digit strings over 0-4 with a random minimum substring length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(3, max(3, min(size, 3000)))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('01234') for _ in range(n))
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [s, k]
